votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the prefix maximum logic and the rolling maximum specification, matching the Coq behavior on all concrete inputs.
The Python code correctly implements the prefix maximum logic and the rolling maximum specification, matching the Coq definitions on all concrete inputs.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq specification, correctly computing the rolling maximum and verifying the result list against the input list.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. max_prefix_at takes the first i+1 elements and returns their maximum (or None if empty), matching Coq's firstn (S i) and fold_left Z.max. rolling_max_spec checks that result has the same length as numbers and that each element equals the corresponding max_prefix_at value, which matches the Coq Prop. The Python uses direct list indexing result[i] which is equivalent to nth_error result i returning Some value when i < length, and the comparison works correctly since max_prefix_at returns the actual max value (not None) for valid indices.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. max_prefix_at takes the first i+1 elements and returns their maximum (or None if empty), matching Coq's firstn (S i) and fold_left Z.max. rolling_max_spec checks that result has the same length as numbers and that each element equals the corresponding max_prefix_at value, which matches the Coq Prop. The only difference is that Python uses result[i] instead of nth_error, but since we iterate over valid indices (i < len(numbers)) and len(result) == len(numbers), this is equivalent for concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. max_prefix_at takes the first i+1 elements and returns their maximum (or None if empty), matching Coq's firstn (S i) and fold_left Z.max. rolling_max_spec checks that result has the same length as numbers and that each element equals the corresponding max_prefix_at value, which matches the Coq Prop. The Python uses direct list indexing result[i] which is equivalent to nth_error result i when i < len(result), and the comparison works correctly since max_prefix_at returns an integer (not None) for valid indices.

Both implementations compute the maximum of the first i+1 elements (returning None/option None when the list is empty) and the spec checks length equality and per-index equality for i < length numbers. The Python code mirrors the Coq behavior on all concrete inputs.
Both implementations compute the maximum over the first i+1 elements (returning None if the list is empty), and the spec checks length equality and per-index agreement for i < len(numbers).

Python max over numbers[:i+1] matches Coq firstn(S i) with fold_left Z.max; rolling_max_spec checks equal lengths and per-index values equal to the prefix max, which corresponds to nth_error equality on in-bounds indices.
